title: Review: The Complexities of DTF: St. Louis
content:
The new television series *DTF: St. Louis* explores the complex consequences of midlife loneliness through the lens of a murder investigation. The plot kicks off when a man is convinced by his friend to download a dating app designed for married locals seeking discreet encounters. When this man is unexpectedly found dead, the app becomes a crucial piece of evidence for two detectives assigned to the case. The veteran detective leans heavily on lazy assumptions, while his young, open-minded partner insists on following the evidence wherever it leads. This dynamic creates a compelling foundation for a procedural drama that is full of unexpected twists.

Premiering early this spring, *DTF: St. Louis* is a tricky show whose apparent inconsistencies actually serve as deliberate artistic choices. It boasts an offbeat, sometimes surreal sense of humor that catches the viewer off guard. The writing and acting alternate seamlessly between stylized stiffness and heart-wrenching realism. For example, the murder victim comes across as a purely kind soul, which makes his willingness to cheat on his wife seem puzzling and out of character. In contrast, the surviving characters often appear to have their authentic selves buried under layers of social artifice.

The creator of the show intentionally hides the perspectives of key characters, leaving the audience guessing about their true motivations. This narrative choice forces viewers to question the reliability of the events unfolding on screen. While some portrayals in the series feel a bit forced, the overarching mystery remains deeply engaging. By blending dark comedy with a genuine exploration of midlife dissatisfaction, the show manages to stand out in a crowded television landscape. Ultimately, it offers a thought-provoking look at the dangerous consequences of seeking escape from an unfulfilling life.
